critique.summary: The article provides a comprehensive cheat sheet for using Claude Code, detailing keyboard shortcuts, server management commands, slash commands, memory and file handling, workflows, configuration options, skills, agents, and CLI flags. It serves as a reference guide for users to efficiently operate the Claude Code environment.
mainPoints: Keyboard shortcuts for general controls, mode switching, input, and session navigation.
Commands for managing MCP servers, including adding servers and listing them.
Various slash commands for session management, configuration, tools, and special functions.
Details on memory and file locations, rules, and auto memory handling.
Workflows and tips including plan mode, thinking and effort toggles, Git worktrees, voice mode, and context management.
Configuration files and environment variables relevant to Claude Code.
Built-in skills and agents with customization options.
CLI commands and flags for interacting with Claude Code from the command line.
critique.truthfulness: The content appears to be a straightforward technical reference or cheat sheet for Claude Code, listing commands and features without making factual claims that require external verification. The information aligns with typical command-line interface documentation and developer tool references. There are no obvious inaccuracies or misleading statements, but the source is not a recognized journalistic outlet and does not cite external sources.
considerations: The article does not provide context about Claude Code's purpose, target audience, or how it compares to similar tools.
No information is given about the reliability, security, or privacy implications of using these commands and features.
There is no mention of the software's version or update history, which could affect command availability or behavior.
The article lacks examples or explanations for how to use the commands effectively in real scenarios.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around a cheat sheet for Claude Code, with users sharing their experiences, suggestions, and critiques about its usability, features, and the need for such documentation. Some praise the resource's utility and automation, while others question the necessity of cryptic commands and suggest improvements like dark mode.

comments.author: phasE89
comments.text:
I use Claude Code daily but kept forgetting commands, so I had Claude research every feature from the docs and GitHub, then generate a printable A4 landscape HTML page covering keyboard shortcuts, slash commands, workflows, skills system, memory/CLAUDE.md, MCP setup, CLI flags, and config files.

It's a single HTML file - Claude wrote it and I iterated on the layout. A daily cron job checks the changelog and updates the sheet automatically, tagging new features with a "NEW" badge.

Auto-detects Mac/Windows for the right shortcuts. Shows current Claude Code version and a dismissable changelog of recent changes at the top.

It will always be lightweight, free, no signup required: https://cc.storyfox.cz

Ctrl+P to print. Works on mobile too.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, practical solution to the problem of remembering commands, including automation and accessibility features, indicating a well-informed and constructive contribution.

comments.author: comboy
comments.text:
Wow /insights is genuinely useful, perhaps CLI should be pushing that as a tip, if one has enough sessions, instead of keep nagging me about the frontend developer skill which I already have installed

In general CLI could be more reliable and responsive though, it's a text based env yet sometimes feel like running windows 95 on 386dx

It seems clear from the insights that some model is marking failure cases when things went wrong and likely reporting home, so that should be extremely valuable to Anthropic
comments.analysis: The comment offers a thoughtful critique of the CLI's user experience and suggests improvements, reflecting practical user insight and constructive feedback.

comments.author: bibimsz
comments.text: Thanks for putting this together! It's really nice to have a quick reference of all the features at a glance — especially since new features are being added all the time. Saves a lot of digging through docs.
comments.analysis: This comment positively acknowledges the value of the cheat sheet as a helpful resource, reinforcing its usefulness without adding technical details.

comments.author: kxrm
comments.text:
CMD + V to paste an image is wrong.

On Mac it's the same as Windows, CTRL + V.

You use CMD + V to paste text.
comments.analysis: The comment corrects a specific technical detail about keyboard shortcuts, providing accurate and useful information for users.

comments.author: guessmyname
comments.text:
There’s actually a lot more environment variables:

edit: removed obnoxious list in favor of the link that @thehamkercat shared below.

My favorite is IS_DEMO=1 to remove a little bit of the unnecessary welcome banner.
comments.analysis: This comment adds valuable information about environment variables, enhancing the discussion with practical tips.

comments.author: vasco
comments.text: Just ask it, this is not needed
comments.analysis: The comment suggests that direct interaction with the AI may reduce the need for cheat sheets, offering a valid perspective on usability.

comments.author: zmmmmm
comments.text: If only there was some kind of tool that could answer helpful questions about technology instead of needing a cheat sheet.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses frustration about the need for cheat sheets, implying a desire for more intuitive or interactive help tools.

comments.author: dangoodmanUT
comments.text: I think this is the argument for UIs - it should be self-explanatory since it's singificantly simpler than an IDE
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully argues for better user interfaces to reduce reliance on cheat sheets, highlighting UX considerations.

comments.author: ninininino
comments.text:
This just exposes why UI like Codex, Cursor, T3 Code, Conductor, Intent, etc are necessary.

This is a bit intense.
comments.analysis: This comment points out the complexity of the CLI and the need for more user-friendly interfaces, contributing a relevant perspective.

comments.author: whalesalad
comments.analysis: A brief but constructive suggestion to improve usability by adding a dark mode.

comments.author: bobjordan
comments.text: Surprised that my most used flag `--dangerously-skip-permissions` is not on it
comments.analysis: This comment highlights a potentially important omission in the cheat sheet, indicating user experience gaps.

comments.author: apoorvdarshan
comments.text: dangerously skip permission is all u need
comments.analysis: This comment is an emotional exaggeration emphasizing the importance of a specific flag but lacks nuance.

comments.author: mrtz
comments.analysis: A polite but minimal comment expressing gratitude without adding substantive content.

comments.author: bingemaker
comments.text: Nice work. Under "MCP" section, "Local" shouldn't be prepended with "~". It should just be `.claude.json (per project)`
comments.analysis: This comment provides a precise correction to the documentation, improving accuracy.

comments.author: amai
comments.text: Why do we still need cryptic commands for an AI?
comments.analysis: Raises a valid UX concern about the complexity of commands but is phrased as a rhetorical question.

comments.author: dirteater_
comments.analysis: A brief but useful addition to the cheat sheet content.

comments.author: williamcotton
comments.text:
Undo (typing):

  Ctrl + _ (Ctrl + underscore)
Applies to the line editor outside of CC as well.
comments.analysis: Provides a helpful keyboard shortcut that enhances user knowledge.

comments.author: hooloovoo_zoo
comments.text: Proposition: Every power user feature added lowers Anthropic’s market cap $1B and OpenAI’s $10B.
comments.analysis: This comment is likely a sarcastic or humorous remark with no factual basis.

comments.author: nizsle
comments.text: I was told the hot new programming language was English
comments.analysis: A sarcastic comment that does not contribute meaningfully to the discussion.
